identity.title: Investigation and Personal Background Check Services

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced employment, tenant, volunteer, vendor, or other personal background screening; continuous workforce monitoring; and repeat corporate, insurance, fraud, due-diligence, or legal investigation services to external organizations.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off domestic, infidelity, missing-person, or other personal assignments; predominantly episodic individual-client detective work; captive employer loss-prevention or internal investigation units; credit bureaus classified in NAICS 561450; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Employers, property managers, insurers, law firms, financial institutions, nonprofits, and other organizations buy reports, verifications, monitoring, or investigations under per-check, subscription, retainer, hourly, or project pricing, with recurring screening volumes and repeat enterprise relationships.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 561611 combines scalable records-based personal background screening with field-heavy private detective and investigative assignments. The lens retains recurring screening and repeat organizational investigation workflows and excludes episodic personal-case work so that data retrieval, verification, exception handling, field investigation, and accountable reporting form a coherent external-service model; the narrowing is for business-model coherence rather than attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe the broader private-detective and investigator occupation, not a wage-weighted task study of recurring 561611 firms. I map database and public-record search, cross-source comparison, routine verification, case summarization, report drafting, and client-status communication to remaining AI exposure, while leaving interviews, surveillance, fingerprint capture, source escalation, legal interpretation, identity resolution, dispute investigation, and evidentiary judgment largely unexposed. The occupation includes captive retail loss prevention and episodic field work, which may understate exposure for screening firms, while screening workflows already automate many easy searches, which may overstate exposure from the published task list; the net error direction is uncertain.
a.rationale: Records retrieval, data normalization, entity matching assistance, document review, and first-draft reporting are digitally tractable. The estimate is below a majority of labor because current screening platforms already automate basic searches, while difficult matches, interviews, field observation, adverse-information verification, disputes, and legally defensible conclusions require human work.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study from PBSA member screening firms and repeat corporate-investigation providers that separates already automated work from manual database search, verification, adjudication support, disputes, fieldwork, and reporting would materially change this value.
caveats: The cited occupation is broader than the industry lens and includes investigators employed in retail, finance, government, and other industries.
No source quantifies the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market screening and investigation firms.
Exposure differs sharply between high-volume background screening and surveillance- or interview-heavy investigations.

rho.bridge: CFPB documents existing third-party background dossiers, algorithmic scores, and technology-enabled worker assessment, while FTC and CFPB specify accuracy, file-access, and dispute obligations for consumer reporting agencies. I translate existing digital delivery and algorithmic products into five-year implementation of the exposed internal workflow, then haircut for fragmented court data, false matches, expunged or sealed records, model validation, customer integration, security, and mandatory human exception handling. Evidence from technology-enabled products may overstate implementation at smaller firms, while standardized per-check workflows may allow faster diffusion than the broader investigator occupation.
rho.rationale: Recurring screening already uses digital data and workflow systems, making assisted search, matching, triage, summarization, and customer-service automation feasible within five years. Implementation is constrained by accuracy duties, source variability, audit trails, privacy and security controls, dispute investigations, customer-specific rules, and the asymmetric cost of a false match or missed record.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent screening and investigation firms reporting workflow-level AI deployment, predeployment automation, exception and false-match rates, dispute volumes, integration cost, human review rates, and realized hours saved would materially change this value.
caveats: Regulatory evidence establishes constraints and existing algorithmic products, not representative adoption or realized labor savings.
Large technology-enabled screening providers may implement faster than lower-middle-market firms with legacy systems.
Field investigation and manual source verification have lower implementation potential than standardized database screening.

e.rationale: The Census definition includes fingerprint, polygraph, private detective, private investigative, and personal background-check services, so the code spans both repeat organizational work and episodic personal cases. Scaled firms in the injected lower-middle-market band are more likely than sole practitioners to have employer, property-manager, insurer, legal, or corporate accounts, but no published firm-level activity or recurrence mix was found.
e.change_evidence: A commercial roster of 561611 firms matched to tax records and coded by customer type, recurring revenue share, screening versus detective revenue, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, and contract transferability would directly establish the eligible share.
caveats: The Census definition lists activities but provides no establishment, revenue, customer, or recurrence shares.
CFPB states that hundreds of companies provide employment background checks but does not map them to NAICS 561611 or the EBITDA band.
The injected count of 46 depends on a broad Business and Consumer Services margin assumption that may not represent either screening platforms or field-investigation firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control acquisitions of 561611 firms. I haircut the reported 22% intention rate for non-closing intentions, family or internal transfers, failed processes, owner-dependent licenses and relationships, and nontransferable investigative know-how. The broad employer population likely overstates qualifying external control transfers where the principal investigator is central, but may understate interest in recurring screening firms with systems and enterprise contracts.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates some prospective supply, but a qualifying transaction must preserve licenses, data access, compliance systems, customer trust, and key investigators. Those dependencies and the gap between plans and completed sales justify a base well below the broad stated-intention rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, PBSA members, and investigation-business brokers, matched to an eligible firm roster and distinguishing external control deals from internal transfers and asset-only closures, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
Licensing, data-provider contracts, customer approvals, and owner reputation may limit transferability.
No industry-specific owner-age or completed-deal series was found.

q.rationale: Per-check, subscription, retainer, and fixed-project pricing can let providers retain near-term savings from automated retrieval, triage, report assembly, and customer service. Retention erodes as enterprise customers rebid contracts, compare API-enabled providers, demand faster turnaround or lower unit prices, and as data-vendor, security, insurance, compliance, and skilled-review costs absorb part of the gross benefit. Hourly investigative work transmits time savings to customers more directly.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of customer contracts, invoices, renewal bids, data-vendor agreements, AI and compliance costs, and gross margins before and after workflow automation, segmented by per-check, subscription, retainer, fixed-fee, and hourly pricing, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits in independent background-screening or investigation firms.
Pricing and pass-through differ materially between standardized checks and hourly bespoke investigations.
Data-source fees, cyber controls, accuracy review, and dispute handling may absorb savings that a labor-only view would overstate.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 6% employment growth for private detectives and investigators from 2024 to 2034 and says lawsuits, fraud, crime, interpersonal mistrust, and continuing background-check work support demand; CFPB describes common employer purchase of consumer reports and expansion into current-worker assessments. I translate these occupation- and regulatory-market indicators into modest five-year constant-price service-quantity growth for the narrower recurring outsourced lens, with a wide downside for hiring cycles, insourcing, and automation-driven service compression. Occupational employment includes workers outside 561611 and can move differently from purchased service quantity, so the direction may overstate or understate industry demand.
d5.rationale: Hiring, tenant turnover, fraud, litigation, vendor risk, and workforce monitoring sustain repeat screening and investigation demand. Growth is likely moderate because employment screening is cyclical, some customers can self-serve public data, and automation reduces the labor content and price of a check even as the number and scope of checks expand.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price US screening volumes and recurring investigation revenue from PBSA surveys, public-provider filings, major data vendors, and customer procurement records, segmented by employment, tenant, insurance, legal, and corporate use, would materially change this value.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation across multiple employing industries rather than outsourced 561611 service quantity.
The demand outlook mixes hiring-sensitive screening with less cyclical fraud, legal, tenant, and monitoring work.
Reported revenue growth could reflect data costs, inflation, or product mix rather than constant-quality service quantity.

o.bridge: FTC and CFPB impose accuracy, file-access, dispute, permissible-purpose, and adverse-action-related obligations on third-party consumer reporting companies, while BLS describes licensed investigators who lawfully collect and document evidence. I translate these obligations and tasks into the share of year-five quantity still needing an accountable screening or investigation operator after software and customer self-service. Regulation may overstate third-party necessity because customers can use first-party data or compliant software, while litigation risk and fragmented records may make accountable review more persistent than the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can automate searches, assemble dossiers, and score records, but a responsible provider still must manage lawful data access, validate identity and record status, investigate disputes, document sources, apply customer rules, and stand behind difficult findings. Field surveillance, interviews, fingerprint services, and evidentiary investigations remain operator-led; routine low-risk checks face the greatest self-service and platform substitution.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement policies, FCRA enforcement data, dispute-resolution staffing, insurer requirements, and contract terms showing which screening or investigation outputs may be accepted without human review or third-party responsibility would materially change this share.
caveats: FCRA obligations do not require every customer to use an independent screening firm.
Private-investigator licensing varies by state and applies unevenly to database-based screening.
Routine checks may migrate to self-service software even while regulated accountability remains with a consumer reporting agency.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent recurring opportunity within 561611 is enterprise background screening, workforce monitoring, and repeat corporate investigation. AI can accelerate records search, matching assistance, exception triage, case summarization, report production, and customer service, but fragmented source data, false-match risk, disputes, privacy law, licensing, and evidentiary standards preserve a substantial accountable human layer.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can normalize names and records, prioritize likely matches, compare sources, identify missing fields, summarize case files, draft reports, route disputes, and support customer communication. Investigators and screening specialists still verify identity and record disposition, resolve conflicting data, conduct interviews or surveillance, assess legal and customer rules, document sources, and approve consequential findings.
narrative.value_capture: Per-check, subscription, retainer, and fixed-fee work can retain productivity gains until enterprise rebids and competitive APIs transmit savings to customers. Hourly investigations pass time savings through more quickly, while data access, model validation, cybersecurity, insurance, compliance, and dispute handling consume part of the benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: The code contains both scaled screening organizations and owner-dependent detective practices. LMM-sized firms are plausibly more weighted toward repeat organizational customers than the establishment population, but no firm-level activity mix exists; licensing, customer trust, data contracts, and key-person knowledge can make otherwise willing sellers difficult to transfer.
narrative.demand_durability: Hiring, tenant turnover, fraud, litigation, vendor diligence, and ongoing worker assessment support repeat demand. BLS expects investigator employment to grow and background checks to remain a source of work, but hiring cycles, customer insourcing, cheaper software, and automated low-risk checks create a meaningful downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the screening-versus-detective revenue mix, current automation baseline, false-match and dispute rates, state privacy and licensing variation, data-vendor dependency, cyber risk, customer concentration, hourly billing exposure, and the margin-bridged injected firm count. Algorithmic outputs can expand compliance obligations rather than remove them.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured records work and repeated report workflows are amenable to AI while regulated accuracy and exception resolution preserve an accountable service role.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code mixes scalable recurring screening with episodic owner-dependent investigation, leaving the eligible firm share and transferable workflow mix unobserved.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 561611
sources.supports: Defines 561611 as investigation, detective, and personal background check services; lists fingerprint, private detective, polygraph, and private investigative services; and excludes credit checks to 561450.

sources.title: Private Detectives and Investigators — Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Describes records search, interviews, surveillance, reporting, lawful evidence collection, licensing, and a 6% employment-growth projection for 2024-2034; states that background checks should remain a source of work.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Private Detectives and Investigators
sources.supports: Lists report writing, database and public-record search, personal background investigations, analysis, interviews, surveillance, and use of advanced technology among investigator tasks.

sources.title: What Employment Background Screening Companies Need to Know About the Fair Credit Reporting Act
sources.supports: Explains that covered screening companies must assure accuracy, obtain client certifications, provide required information, give file access, and reasonably investigate consumer disputes.

sources.title: Fair Credit Reporting; Background Screening
sources.supports: States that consumer reporting agencies using public records need procedures preventing duplicative, expunged, sealed, or otherwise restricted information to satisfy maximum-possible-accuracy duties.

sources.title: CFPB Circular 2024-06: Background Dossiers and Algorithmic Scores
sources.supports: Documents common employer purchase of background dossiers, growth in third-party technology and worker assessments, algorithmic scores, and continuing FCRA accuracy, disclosure, and dispute obligations.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in a fall 2024 survey and notes transfer challenges for owner-dependent firms.

sources.title: When I Apply for a Job, What Do Employers See in a Background Check?
sources.supports: States that hundreds of companies provide employment background checks as consumer reporting agencies and that many employers also use third-party reports for current workers.
